messages.content:
It seems there is a wrong type of double quote character (“”) in your code. You should use the correct double quote character (") for the strings. Please change the double quote characters and try again.

Here's the corrected code:

```kotlin
import java.text.SimpleDateFormat
import java.util.Calendar
import java.util.Locale
import java.util.Date

fun main() {
    println(currentWeek())
}

fun currentWeek(): String {
    val currentDate = Calendar.getInstance()
    val weekNum = currentDate[Calendar.WEEK_OF_YEAR]
    val dateFormat = SimpleDateFormat("Week_" + weekNum + "_MMM_yyyy", Locale.getDefault())
    return dateFormat.format(currentDate.time)
}
```

This code should work without the `java.lang.IllegalArgumentException`.

is there anyway to add a successful and failure listener for all of those queries?

Yes, you can add success and failure listeners to each query. In order to do that, you can use the `addOnSuccessListener` and `addOnFailureListener` methods to your write operations.

You can create a function that handles success and failure listeners:

```kotlin
import com.google.android.gms.tasks.Task
import java.lang.Exception

private fun handleTask(task: Task<Void>, successMessage: String, failureMessage: String) {
    task.addOnSuccessListener {
        Log.d("TAG", successMessage)
    }.addOnFailureListener { exception ->
        Log.e("TAG", failureMessage, exception)
    }
}
```

Then, you can modify your write operations to use this function:

```kotlin
val yearlyTask = db.collection("expenses")
    .document(userId)
    .collection("yearly")
    .document(currentDate[Calendar.YEAR].toString())
    .set(initData)

val monthlyTask = db.collection("expenses")
    .document(userId)
    .collection("monthly")
    .document(currentMonth())
    .set(initData)

val weeklyTask = db.collection("expenses")
    .document(userId)
    .collection("weekly")
    .document(currentWeek())
    .set(initData)

val dailyTask = db.collection("expenses")
    .document(userId)
    .collection("daily")
    .document(currentDay())
    .set(initData)

handleTask(yearlyTask, "Yearly data saved successfully", "Error saving yearly data")
handleTask(monthlyTask, "Monthly data saved successfully", "Error saving monthly data")
handleTask(weeklyTask, "Weekly data saved successfully", "Error saving weekly data")
handleTask(dailyTask, "Daily data saved successfully", "Error saving daily data")
```

This code will add success and failure listeners to each of your write operations, and you will see logs for success and failure events. Please make sure to have imported the required classes in your file.

when I update the document that have the same name it will replace the expenses and quantity what I want instead is it will do an addition if its the same name, and create a new field if its a new name, I think we need to use array in the firestore field

Yes, you can use Firestore transactions or FieldValue.increment() to perform this update operation. I would suggest using FieldValue.increment(), as it is simpler and easier to use. Here's how you can update the document with the increment.

Assuming you have fields "expenses" and "quantity" in your document, this code will perform the addition if the fields already exist or add new fields if they don't:

```kotlin
import com.google.firebase.firestore.FieldValue
import com.google.firebase.firestore.FirebaseFirestore

val db = FirebaseFirestore.getInstance()
val expensesRef = db.collection("expenses").document(userId)

val newData = hashMapOf(
    "expenses" to FieldValue.increment(1),
    "quantity" to FieldValue.increment(1)
)

// Performs addition if the fields already exist or creates new fields if they don't.
expensesRef.update(newData)
    .addOnSuccessListener {
        Log.d("TAG", "Document updated successfully")
    }
    .addOnFailureListener { exception ->
        Log.e("TAG", "Error updating document", exception)
    }
```

Remember to replace `1` with the desired values for updating "expenses" and "quantity".

no error but there is no update happening

The error message indicates a type mismatch in the `updateData`. You can explicitly cast the `updateData` variable to type `Map<String, Any>` to resolve the issue. Here's the updated code:

```kotlin
val updateData: Map<String, Any> = hashMapOf(
    "name" to names,
    "quantity" to quantities,
    "expenses" to expenses
)

docRef.update(updateData)
    .addOnSuccessListener {
        Log.d("TAG", "Document updated successfully")
    }
    .addOnFailureListener { exception ->
        Log.e("TAG", "Error updating document", exception)
    }
```

This should resolve the type mismatch issue and update the document correctly.

There seems to be a minor oversight in the code. In the `updateData` HashMap, you're currently adding the original (non-mutable) lists `names`, `quantities`, and `expenses`. You should be adding their mutable versions instead, which have the updated values.

Here's the corrected code snippet:

```kotlin
val updateData: Map<String, Any> = hashMapOf(
    "name" to mutableNames,
    "quantity" to mutableQuantities,
    "expenses" to mutableExpenses
)

docRef.update(updateData)
    .addOnSuccessListener {
        Log.d("TAG", "Document updated successfully")
    }
    .addOnFailureListener { exception ->
        Log.e("TAG", "Error updating document", exception)
    }
```

This should now correctly update the document in Firestore.
I added a fourth field which is a number type and it will be the sum of expenses across all the value in expenses array

You can add another field, for example, "totalExpenses", which will hold the sum of all expenses in the "expenses" array. You can calculate the sum before creating the `updateData` HashMap and include it in the HashMap.

Here's how you can integrate the "totalExpenses" field into the existing code snippet:

```kotlin
// ...
val mutableNames = names.toMutableList()
val mutableQuantities = quantities.toMutableList()
val mutableExpenses = expenses.toMutableList()

// ...

// Calculate the total expenses
val totalExpenses = mutableExpenses.sum()

val updateData: Map<String, Any> = hashMapOf(
    "name" to mutableNames,
    "quantity" to mutableQuantities,
    "expenses" to mutableExpenses,
    "totalExpenses" to totalExpenses
)

docRef.update(updateData)
    .addOnSuccessListener {
        Log.d("TAG", "Document updated successfully")
    }
    .addOnFailureListener { exception ->
        Log.e("TAG", "Error updating document", exception)
    }
```

With this update, the document will include a new field named "totalExpenses", which will hold the sum of all expenses in the "expenses" array. The sum() function calculates the total expenses using the updated values in the mutableExpenses list.
